author: @handyhippie6548
text: the large wheels from an old wheelchair would be a good source for something to attach your blades to that have fairly good sealed ball bearings in the hubs. and since most have plastic rims, you should be able to glue the plastic blades directly to the rims. plus the remainder of the chair has many other parts that are useful like the large caster wheels, the vinyl or cloth seat and back, and tube frame. i got over thirty of them for free from a nursing home that was disposing of chairs that previously belonged to deceased patients that i have made many things from. one of them are folding camp/work stools i made for myself and my friends from the part of the frame the seat is attached to on folding wheelchairs. it has an x joint where it folds together already. i cut feet from the vertical tubing that the back is fastened to with a cut off disk on an angle grinder, and attach them to the u shaped bottom pivots(a source of thin sheet brass for shim stock) with hardware that i removed from the chair. presto! a free and really strong folding stool.

replies.author: @Alienami
replies.text:
I liked the idea, but his plan relies on two almost identical ones, or you get wobble.
If you made a more traditional horizontal fan style, you would only need one, and that would be fine. 

I would suggest using bicycle wheels, as they more are universally found, for this drum style. 

And for the blades use duct tape (or sheets of linen cut to size) coated in a Resin, moulded over a pipe (protected by cling wrap)... That way you have a reliable and cheaper source of blades.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
on bearings I would think that two angle bearings would be better, (angled cup and beveled insert to match with cylindrical ball bearings (ie not spherical so technically not "ball" bearings) that roll over that flat surface and is trapped by the angled insert, with contact on outside and bottom on bottom one, and outside and top (flip them so the angles of both sides of each bearing cup form a diamond shape) that way they self align, too, as well as handling the weight between them extremely efficiently, 

This type you are making (as far as I can tell) would benefit from my idea of a second set of blades that are flat and stationary around the outside that angle so wind coming from any side that falls between two of them is buffeted to the side that pushes your VAWT around, and protects the side that is not using it to push it around, from that wind, as well as increasing the cross section of air flow it will catch and concentrate on the windmill, so it will hopefully perform better than one that actual size since it is not having to lug that weight around, again I will make a better video of that design than I already shared here, as that one I tried to optimize for better air flow, but it just makes it harder to tell what I was going for.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
This is a lot of reading, for some, and might be hard to understand, so if you choose not to read it all and want to see a video of the process instead, leave a reply to my comment, 

something I found useful for rounding that edge of cut PVC (back when I made melee weapons from PVC water pipe and electrical grey PVC conduit, covered in tube insulation, wrapped in duct tape and electrical tape (blade end with silver duct, and handle/cross guards black electric tape.) ) was to use the tip end of the unbroken new snap off box cutter blades on the top back side of it as this segment was always cut away with a round inverted cut for some reason so when you drag that across you would get a round edge (it looked a bit like someone punched through the angled corner with a hole punch for a paper punch sorta, so one side it was super sharp and the other was a bit rounded off from that being the side the punch went through first, so if I dragged it sharp side first to cut off a rounded or curved area around the tip of the tube of PVC it would cut away swarth or rough bits from cutting it with a hack saw, and pipe cutters left a flat sharp edge so I used it on those too, then I would come back and drag it over backwards so the rounded edge of the punched partial hole would smooth off the PVC by just being a round smooth metal surface that refuses to take anything off so the pressure would press down on it nicely, and I had to round it well because a pill of foam wrapped in strapping tape was shoved into a cup made of strapping tape that went over the end of that pipe and dipped down into it, so the pill could rest in it like a person sleeping in a hammock, and protrude a couple inches out of the tip thus ensuring the tip of the outter tube insulation would not allow the tip of the sharp PVC to come through it and the duct tape causing it to hurt someone as people swung these things at each other as if they were a sword/baseball bat all day long in the park LOL 

not sure if that pressing down with the not sharp back portion of the blade to shine and polish up that rounded edge would matter in this case or not, for wind flow. but thought I would drop that here so people can try it out, and might make a quickie video of the process if its not clear enough what I am talking about

author: @tomkelly8827
text:
Hey there Robert, amazing work as usual!
I would like to add in my 2 cents on VAWT's for this community, it is not meant to be critical of your fantastic work, just to add some more information. So if you go for a vertical axis design, magnetic bearing levitation can reduce the friction losses. Also if a tail and a wind blocker is added, the friction from wind on the return side can be minimized, more energy can be harvested from the same wind space. Also less magnets and coils are needed if gearing or belt drives are used to spin up a smaller disc much faster, rather then placing them around the outside of this large disc.

I love your channel and love your making things from garbage approach! Very inspiring! Keep up the good work

author: @ronaldd2154
text:
Excellent work!
 
Forgive me for stating the obvious, and for testing its fine, and probably is why you are using it as time is short. But to anyone following this recipe I'd advise against super glue as a permanent solution. It most likely wont hold up well to the elements. Epoxy would be better, but buying cheap, flat, little angle brackets which can be bolted or self tapping screws to the base and the fins would be much more reliable, also giving them a coat of paint so they won't rust👍. 

If anyone dare calls this a "Toy" I will slap them 🤣👍👍.

No doubt in my mind it will have a good, useful output and with a few tweaks here and there truly make it a serious, reliable, affordable, robust piece of tech which will last many many years. 

Truly well done Sir👍

author: @pencilguin
text:
Hello Robert, er, Rob? Mr. Murray-Smith? New subscriber, first time posting here :). Three things, two of which are too late.
1) I would have placed the chair bases on the outside to help ensure clean airflow through the turbine. (Assuming the attachments were just as easy).
2) I think I would have slotted the little pipe pieces to hold each blade on both sides at the proper angle.
3) Obviously, mounting the magnets at the greatest diameter is the best - but there are several other possibilities that might be interesting. For example, you could use the edge of the disc, either instead, or in addition. But a second, or n more rows of magnets inside the greatest diameter could still be useful. Also, if you get enough magnets and coils in play to cause significant drag, then you could consider a mechanism to change the distance of the coil to the magnet, to keep the speed constant, which may get you the most power out in the long run.
Keep up the great work! Thanks!

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
That does look very solid. If there was any consistent kind of wind here in Durban I'd probably be building myself one of those eventually.

If your drum is out of square slightly (Which you say it isn't, at 1mm), you might consider putting the magnets in a perpendicular arrangement around the circumference and have your transformer core vertically at the bottom. That way you could use your pen trick to get an exact mounting ring, and it'd probably be more resistant to flexing if that's a problem. One difficulty with that is you'd need to be exact in your mounting instead of relying on the thickness of the magnets.

Either way, I really like where this is going.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: im surprised that you dont get a lot of wind being the "blackpool' of South Africa ^_~

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text:
​ @drop stix  Great idea. Are you talking about making the face flat, or the circumference round? As J W is concerned, I'd worry about flex pulling the circumference inward as the blades bow outwards at speed, increasing the gap to the magnets if they were mounted onto the flat face. If they were mounted around the outside you'd get more consistent, but you'd probably have to modify the transformer core to be concave to take the magnets as close as possible.

​@paulmaydaynight9925 Yeah, we hardly get any extreme weather (apart from a bit of heat sometimes). I think it's because we have a pretty consistently hot ocean temperature flowing from up north. Further south there's a lot more wind, where there are two ocean currents nearby of rather different temperatures.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Since you want a good, stable, solid system, MY suggestion is to face them where one can drive a piece of all-thread through all 5 spokes of the office chair legs and with a couple of extra nuts, be able to ensure a very strong, rigid, flat, parallel, uniform pair of disks.

ADMITTEDLY, since all-thread DOES cost  bit of money, if it is going to make the project cost too much, then I can see one leaving it out for that reason, however, just knowing wind the way I do, it seems to me a pretty near-to-being-necessary component.

If at some point you find the disks are for some reason failing to maintain their shape in strong wind, you can just take them out, make new pieces, and go on in with the all-thread, but I suppose that until it is clear that you will need it, may as well leave it off.  ~( 'w')/

I was GOING to make a comment about weather sealing, but then I saw that you had gone ahead and done so.
